12: Corinth was one of the most commercially and socially stratified cities in Greece — a Roman colony dominated by social climbing (cursus honorum), patron-client relationships, and competitive display of status. The factions around Apollos, Cephas, Paul (and a 'Christ' party, possibly anti-leader) reflect the patron-client pattern of the ancient city: clients attached themselves publicly to powerful figures. Apollos is specifically described as 'eloquent' (logios, Acts 18:24) and trained in Alexandria — the preeminent philosophical and rhetorical city. The Corinthian preference for Apollos over Paul reflects the Greco-Roman premium on paideia (rhetorical education) and philosophical sophistication.
26: 'Not many of you were wise according to worldly standards, not many were powerful, not many were of noble birth' — Paul's sociological observation about the Corinthian congregation aligns with archaeological and literary evidence about early Christianity. The preponderance of craftsmen, freedpersons, and modest household-level patrons (Stephanas, Gaius — who could host the whole church, 1 Corinthians 16:15; Romans 16:23) in the Pauline churches suggests a largely sub-elite membership, with some wealthy exceptions. Theissen's 'social-level theory' of the Corinthian congregation has been refined but not overturned: the community spanned a wider social range than most ancient voluntary associations, creating the tensions Paul addresses (especially in 1 Corinthians 11's Lord's Supper debate).
1: The Pauline letter-formula follows the standard Greek epistolary convention: sender, recipient, greeting (chairein). Paul adapts it with theological freight: his apostleship is klētos apostolos (called apostle) — not self-appointed but divinely commissioned. Sosthenes appears in Acts 18:17 as the synagogue ruler beaten before Gallio; if this is the same person, his co-authorship signals his subsequent conversion and standing in the Corinthian community.
2: Corinth's church (ekklēsia) — a term carrying both Jewish-synagogue resonance (the LXX rendering of qahal, the assembly of Israel) and Greco-Roman political resonance (the ekklēsia was the citizen assembly). Paul's audience would hear the term as simultaneously Jewish covenant-assembly and civic assembly. Hēgiasmenois en Christō Iēsou (sanctified in Christ Jesus): perfect passive participle — a completed act of consecration establishing their current standing. The address extends to panta topō (every place) — the letter circulates beyond Corinth.
3: The greeting charis kai eirēnē (grace and peace) blends the Greek epistolary chairein (greetings) with the Jewish shalom — a bilingual greeting that signals a community spanning ethnic boundaries. Paul uses this formula in every letter; its origin is likely his own liturgical practice adapted from Jewish synagogue greetings.
4: Thanksgiving sections (eucharistō) were standard in Greco-Roman letters but Paul fills his with theological content. The thanks is directed peri hymōn (concerning you) — personalized, not formulaic. The ground is epi tē chariti tou Theou (because of the grace of God): grace is the initiating divine act, not the Corinthians' achievement. This sets the tone for the entire letter's confrontation with Corinthian pride.
5: Enriched en panti logō kai pasē gnōsei (in all speech and all knowledge) — Paul acknowledges the Corinthians' genuine gifts before critiquing their misuse. Logos and gnōsis are precisely the capacities the Corinthians prized and competed over. The thanksgiving grants them as genuine divine endowments, which sharpens the subsequent critique: the problem is not that they lack gifts but that they misuse them.
6: The testimony of Christ being confirmed among them recalls Paul's founding preaching mission in Acts 18:1-18: Paul stayed 18 months in Corinth (1 Corinthians 1:11), establishing a community that itself became evidence of the gospel's power. The Corinthian community is a living confirmation of the apostolic witness.
7: Hystereisthe (you do not lack) — present tense: the gifts are currently operative. Charisma (spiritual gift): Paul uses this term later in detail (1 Corinthians 12-14); here he anticipates the full treatment. Apokalypsin (revelation/apocalypse of our Lord Jesus Christ): the eschatological horizon shapes all Pauline ethics; the community awaits the Lord's unveiling, which will also be the Day of judgment (1 Corinthians 1:8).
8: Heos telous (to the end) — divine faithfulness guarantees perseverance to the eschatological consummation. Anengklētous en tē hēmera tou Kyriou (blameless on the Day of the Lord): the Day of the Lord language comes from the Hebrew prophets (Amos 5:18-20; Joel 2:1); Paul applies it to the Parousia of Jesus. The concern about blamelessness on that Day becomes the context for all of Paul's ethical teaching in the letter.
9: Pistos ho Theos (God is faithful) — the bedrock theological claim. In Jewish tradition, divine faithfulness (emunah/pistis) is God's covenant-keeping reliability: what he promises, he performs. The call into koinōnia (fellowship, partnership) with his Son is Paul's first use of the koinōnia term, which becomes central to his understanding of the Lord's Supper (1 Corinthians 10:16-17).
10: Parakalō (I appeal, I urge) — the standard Pauline paraenetic verb, carrying the weight of apostolic exhortation without the authoritative command (parangellō). Dia tou onomatos tou Kyriou Iēsou Christou (in the name of the Lord Jesus Christ): the appeal is in the name, invoking Christ's authority for the unity request. Katērtismenoi (perfectly joined, completely united) — the technical term for mending fishing nets or setting a fractured bone: the community needs repair, not merely encouragement.
11: Chloe's household (tōn Chloes): Chloe is mentioned by name — a woman who owned a household large enough to have representatives traveling between Corinth and Ephesus. This is among the earliest evidence for women's leadership roles in Pauline communities. The eris (quarrels, contentions) reported by her household is the concrete problem Paul addresses: factional rivalry structured around patron-client allegiances to different apostolic figures.
13: Three rhetorical questions attack the absurdity of the factions: Was Christ divided? Was Paul crucified for you? Were you baptized into Paul's name? Baptism in the ancient world created an allegiance-bond — to be baptized into someone's name was to become their property/client. Paul denies the factions any theological legitimacy by pointing to the exclusive claims of Christ's crucifixion and baptism: both belong to Christ alone, not to apostolic factions.
14: Crispus was the Corinthian synagogue ruler who converted (Acts 18:8) — a significant patron-figure whose conversion would have been socially and financially consequential. Gaius hosted the entire Corinthian church (Romans 16:23) — clearly a man of means with a house large enough for the assembly. Paul's relief at not having baptized them himself reflects his concern that personal baptism creates personal loyalty bonds that would fuel the faction problem.
15: The danger Paul avoids: if he had baptized many in Corinth, his followers could claim special status as Paul's clients through the baptismal bond. The eis to emon onoma (into my name) would have been precisely the language of Greco-Roman religious initiation and patron-client allegiance. Paul's sparse baptizing practice is a deliberate counter to patronal religion.
16: The parenthetical admission (yes, I also baptized the household of Stephanas) is a genuine aside — Paul is thinking aloud rather than composing a formal argument. Stephanas is named three times in 1 Corinthians (1 Corinthians 1:16; 1 Corinthians 16:15, 17) as a founding convert and patron-figure. Paul's forgetfulness about other baptisms suggests he delegated most baptisms to co-workers — consistent with his practice in Acts (1 Corinthians 20:4; Romans 16:1-16).
17: Euaggelizesthai (to preach the gospel): Paul's self-description of his primary calling. The phrase ouk en sophia logou (not with wisdom of words) is a reference to Sophistic rhetoric, which Paul systematically avoided in Corinth. The Sophists competed for fees and prestige by displaying their eloquence (logos); Paul's deliberate refusal of their style was a counter-cultural statement that the message itself carried the power, not the messenger's rhetorical artistry. His arrival in Corinth after Athens (Acts 17:32-18:1) may have sharpened this decision.
18: The cross as social shame: crucifixion was used by Romans specifically for slaves, rebels, and enemies of the state — the most dishonorable death possible in the Roman world. Cicero wrote that the very word crux (cross) should be kept far from the eyes and ears of Roman citizens (Pro Rabirio 5.16). Jewish sensibility added the Deuteronomic curse: anyone hung on a tree is cursed by God (Deuteronomy 21:23, which Paul applies explicitly in Galatians 3:13). To preach a crucified savior was to preach a shamed, defeated, cursed figure — precisely what Paul calls mōria (foolishness) to Greeks and skandalon (stumbling block, scandal) to Jews.
19: Paul cites Isaiah 29:14 (LXX): God will destroy the wisdom of the wise and nullify the intelligence of the intelligent. Isaiah's original context is God's judgment on Jerusalem's advisors who sought political alliance with Egypt instead of trust in YHWH. Paul applies it to the Corinthian wisdom-competition: the same divine pattern — God overturning human strategic cleverness — is now operative in the gospel.
20: Three social-philosophical figures: sophos (the philosopher/sage), grammateus (the scribe/Torah scholar, Jewish), syzētētēs (the debater/disputer, Greek rhetor). All three were recognized prestige-figures in their respective communities. Paul asks: where are they? — meaning: the cross has rendered the entire apparatus of human wisdom irrelevant. Ho aion houtos (this age): Paul's eschatological framing — the present age is temporary and its wisdom structures will pass.
21: The sophia tou Theou (wisdom of God) here is not a divine attribute but a divine strategy: God's choice to reveal himself through the cross rather than through philosophical demonstration. Hē mōria tou kērygmatos (the foolishness of the proclamation): the kērygma is the announcement, the proclaimed word — Paul makes the content, not the technique, the object of attack. Natural theology (knowing God through worldly wisdom) has failed; God chose the path of proclamation instead.
22: Jewish expectation of signs (sēmeia) was rooted in the Exodus tradition: Moses validated his mission through miraculous signs (Exodus 4:1-9), and the prophetic tradition expected the messianic age to be authenticated by miracles (Isaiah 35:5-6; John 2:18; Matthew 12:38). Greek pursuit of wisdom (sophia zētousin) reflects the Hellenistic philosophical tradition from Socrates through the Sophists: the educator/philosopher demonstrated the value of his teaching through the quality of his reasoning and rhetoric.
23: A crucified messiah was contradictory in both Jewish and Greek frameworks: the Jewish messiah was expected to be a victorious king-warrior, not a condemned criminal executed by Rome; the Greek ideal of the hero or divine man (theios anēr) was one of power, beauty, and wisdom, not social shame and execution. The skandalon (stumbling block, scandal — also the word for the trigger of an animal trap) is not a minor offense but a complete category-violation. The cross categorically disproves both messianic and divine-hero expectations.
24: But to those called — both Jews and Greeks — the cross is Theou dynamin kai Theou sophian (God's power and God's wisdom). The calling (klētos) is the epistemological key: the cross is only legible as power and wisdom from within the new community constituted by the Spirit. This is not intellectual cowardice but a claim about the social location of genuine understanding: the cross can only be properly read from the inside of the community shaped by the risen Christ.
25: The paradox of divine weakness and divine foolishness: Paul does not claim the cross is secretly wise and powerful by human standards — he claims that God's apparent foolishness (to mōron tou Theou) exceeds all human wisdom, and God's apparent weakness (to asthenes tou Theou) exceeds all human strength. This is a real reversal, not a rhetorical trick: the cross actually defeats death through death, actually brings wisdom through what appears absurd.
27: The deliberate divine selection of the socially marginal is a pattern running from the OT's election of Israel (Deuteronomy 7:7: not because you are the most numerous), through the prophets' preferential option for the poor, into the beatitudes (Matthew 5:3-12). Ta mōra tou kosmou (the foolish things of the world), ta asthene tou kosmou (the weak things), ta agene kai ta exouthenēmena (the lowly and despised) — Paul is describing the actual social composition of the Corinthian church: dominated by artisans, slaves, freedpersons, and sub-elite households.
28: Ta mē onta (the things that are not): Paul's most extreme formulation — God chose even those who do not exist in the reckoning of the powerful. In Greco-Roman status-reckoning, the poor and landless were effectively social non-entities (humiles). Their election as the primary recipients of the gospel is not incidental but structural to God's strategy of abolishing human grounds for boasting.
29: Hopōs mē kauchēsētai pasa sarx enōpion tou Theou (so that no flesh may boast before God): the explicit teleology of divine election. Kauchēsis (boasting) in the Greco-Roman world was not mere personal vanity but the public display of honor credentials — one's lineage, military achievements, rhetorical victories, civic benefactions. Paul's argument denies any such credential access to God: divine favor is entirely grace-given, leaving no room for achievement-boasting.
30: Jeremiah 9:23-24 (LXX) underlies this verse: do not boast in wisdom, strength, or riches — boast only in knowing the Lord. Paul cites it in 1 Corinthians 1:31. The application of Jeremiah's anti-boasting polemic to the specific Corinthian situation of wisdom-rivalry and faction demonstrates Paul's hermeneutic: the same divine logic that overturned Israel's trust in its own resources now overturns the Corinthian trust in rhetorical sophia.
31: The citation of Jeremiah 9:24 closes the argument of 1 Corinthians 1:18-31 with a scriptural capstone. Kauchastho en Kyriō (let the one who boasts boast in the Lord): the only legitimate boast is one directed toward God, acknowledging that all gifts are received. This anti-boasting principle governs the entire letter: chapters 1-4 (wisdom), 5-6 (ethics), 7 (celibacy/marriage), 12-14 (gifts), and 15 (resurrection) all address forms of Corinthian self-congratulation that misappropriate divine gifts as grounds for personal status-claims.
